region_of_origin: Japan (origin); widely produced and consumed in East and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for packaged matcha latte; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) is the closest defensible broader class. A more specific term for sweetened RTD matcha latte is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible category for a packaged sweetened matcha latte. No exact FoodEx2 code for RTD matcha latte exists in the published hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged matcha latte as a food product. SNOMED CT coverage for specific RTD tea beverages is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged matcha latte bottle or carton contains approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing around 113 kcal, 19.95 g sugar, and 52.5 mg sodium per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Matcha Latte (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D09020059 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g values are taken from the HPB record without modification. Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the source and is documented in notes. Saturated fat (0.17 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Beverages, tea, green, ready-to-drink, sweetened' (FDC ID 174907) as the closest approved-source analogue and is flagged as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged matcha latte in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to sweetened milk-based tea beverages (e.g., sweetened milk tea, GI ~50–60) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dominant glycaemic driver is the sugar content (7.98 g/100g); milk protein and fat moderately attenuate the glucose response. GL per 250 g serving = (55 × 24.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.6, rounded to 13.7. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use. GI 55 is classified as 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI ≤ 55 is low, 56–69 is medium, ≥70 is high).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (7.98 g sugar/100 g) with low sodium (21 mg/100 g); SSB consumption independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sympathetic activation and uric acid pathways (AHA 2018).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this packaged sweetened matcha latte and instead choose unsweetened matcha prepared with plain low-fat milk or water, as the 7.98 g/100 g sugar load independently elevates blood pressure risk despite the low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.98 g sugar/100 g and only 0.37 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, producing a sharp postprandial glucose spike with no fibre buffer.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged matcha latte because it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering approximately 19.95 g of sugar per 250 ml serving with negligible fibre, causing rapid postprandial hyperglycaemia; unsweetened matcha powder prepared with unsweetened milk is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular SSB consumption is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk; fat content is low (0.27 g/100 g) and sodium is low (21 mg/100 g), but the sugar load (7.98 g/100 g) is the primary concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or avoid this sweetened packaged matcha latte, as habitual sugar-sweetened beverage intake raises triglycerides and lowers HDL cholesterol; switching to unsweetened matcha eliminates the added sugar burden while retaining the antioxidant EGCG content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.98 g/100 g) with fibre <1 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, negating the anti-inflammatory EGCG polyphenols present in matcha.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to freshly prepared unsweetened matcha to retain the anti-inflammatory EGCG benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar load present in this packaged sweetened latte.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged matcha latte is a ready-to-drink (RTD) beverage made from Japanese green tea powder (matcha) blended with milk or milk solids, water, and added sugar, then commercially heat-treated and sealed in bottles or cartons for retail sale. The drink has a mild, slightly bitter green tea flavour balanced by sweetness and a creamy, smooth texture from the milk component. It is widely available across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore, Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan, and is popular as a convenient breakfast or snack beverage. Per 100 g, it provides 45 kcal, 0.78 g protein, 0.27 g fat, 9.93 g carbohydrate (of which 7.98 g is sugar), 0.37 g dietary fibre, and 21 mg sodium. The low calorie density and low sodium are positive attributes, but the high sugar-to-fibre ratio classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to the rapid glucose absorption from liquid sugar with minimal fibre buffering. The matcha component contains EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), a polyphenol with documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, but the added sugar in the packaged form largely negates this benefit. For health-conscious consumers, unsweetened matcha powder prepared with plain low-fat milk is the recommended alternative.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bottle of matcha latte?
Is matcha latte high in sugar?
Is RTD matcha latte good for weight loss?
What is the GI of matcha latte?

What is the difference between matcha latte and green tea?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020059, review_status 'reviewed', confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and is clearly documented as such. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable sweetened milk-based tea beverages and documented in notes.
notes: 1. Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id D09020059); this is a confirmed zero per the approved source. 2. Saturated fat (0.17 g/100 g) is not reported in the HPB source record; it is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Beverages, tea, green, ready-to-drink, sweetened' (FDC ID 174907) as the closest approved-source analogue, and is flagged as an adapted value. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific packaged matcha latte product are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk-based tea beverages per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI 55 is classified as 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database criteria (low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70). 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical ingredients (matcha, milk, sugar) but is not confirmed by a halal certification body for this specific product; consumers should verify the product label. 6. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input record are retained as contextually accurate (sodium 21 mg/100 g; energy 45 kcal/100 g) but do not override the SSB classification for clinical purposes. 7. This is a Japanese-inspired beverage widely produced internationally; the mandatory Japanese local-language name is provided. Singaporean multilingual requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply as this is not a Singaporean dish; Chinese names are provided as supplementary given the beverage's widespread consumption in Chinese-speaking markets.
